problems.title: Behavior Expectation
problems.description: Behavior Expectation Use the sample setup shown below and match the example output. Implement the logic in main(...) and match the expected output for the provided cases.
problems.prompt: Behavior Expectation Use the sample setup shown below and match the example output.
problems.solution:
def main():
    def behavior_expectation():
        print('\n--- Behavior Expectation ---')
        print("Description: Immutable objects can't be modified, so functions can't change them. Mutable objects can be changed, so functions can alter them.")

        def append_suffix(name):
            return name + '_suffix'
        print('\nFunction: append_suffix')
        original_name = 'deepak'
        print('Input: original_name =', original_name)
        modified_name = append_suffix(original_name)
        print('Output: modified_name =', modified_name)

        def add_item_to_list(lst):
            lst.append('new item')
            return lst
        print('\nFunction: add_item_to_list')
        my_list = ['item1', 'item2']
        print('Input: my_list =', my_list)
        modified_list = add_item_to_list(my_list.copy())
        print('Output: modified_list =', modified_list)

    behavior_expectation()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

examples.output:
--- Behavior Expectation ---
Description: Immutable objects can't be modified, so functions can't change them. Mutable objects can be changed, so functions can alter them.

Function: append_suffix
Input: original_name = deepak
Output: modified_name = deepak_suffix

Function: add_item_to_list
Input: my_list = ['item1', 'item2']
Output: modified_list = ['item1', 'item2', 'new item']

test_cases.expected_output:
--- Behavior Expectation ---
Description: Immutable objects can't be modified, so functions can't change them. Mutable objects can be changed, so functions can alter them.

Function: append_suffix
Input: original_name = deepak
Output: modified_name = deepak_suffix

Function: add_item_to_list
Input: my_list = ['item1', 'item2']
Output: modified_list = ['item1', 'item2', 'new item']
test_cases.label: Test 1

problems.title: Function Side Effects
problems.description: Function Side-Effects Use the sample setup shown below and match the example output. Implement the logic in main(...) and match the expected output for the provided cases.
problems.prompt: Function Side-Effects Use the sample setup shown below and match the example output.
problems.solution:
def main():
    def function_side_effects():
        print('\n--- Function Side-Effects ---')
        print('Description: Functions can have side effects on mutable objects, changing their state.')

        def modify_list(lst):
            lst.append('modified')
            return lst
        print('\nFunction: modify_list')
        my_list = ['original']
        print('Input: my_list =', my_list)
        new_list = modify_list(my_list)
        print('Output: new_list =', new_list)
        print('Original list after modification:', my_list)

    function_side_effects()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

examples.output:
--- Function Side-Effects ---
Description: Functions can have side effects on mutable objects, changing their state.

Function: modify_list
Input: my_list = ['original']
Output: new_list = ['original', 'modified']
Original list after modification: ['original', 'modified']

test_cases.expected_output:
--- Function Side-Effects ---
Description: Functions can have side effects on mutable objects, changing their state.

Function: modify_list
Input: my_list = ['original']
Output: new_list = ['original', 'modified']
Original list after modification: ['original', 'modified']
test_cases.label: Test 1

problems.title: Copying Behavior
problems.description: Copying Behavior Use the sample setup shown below and match the example output. Implement the logic in main(...) and match the expected output for the provided cases.
problems.prompt: Copying Behavior Use the sample setup shown below and match the example output.
problems.solution:
def main():
    def copying_behavior():
        print('\n--- Copying Behavior ---')
        print('Description: Shallow copying of mutable objects allows independent modifications of the copy and original.')
        original_list = [1, 2, 3]
        copied_list = original_list.copy()
        copied_list.append(4)
        print('\nOriginal List:', original_list)
        print('Copied List:', copied_list)

    copying_behavior()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

examples.output:
--- Copying Behavior ---
Description: Shallow copying of mutable objects allows independent modifications of the copy and original.

Original List: [1, 2, 3]
Copied List: [1, 2, 3, 4]

test_cases.expected_output:
--- Copying Behavior ---
Description: Shallow copying of mutable objects allows independent modifications of the copy and original.

Original List: [1, 2, 3]
Copied List: [1, 2, 3, 4]
test_cases.label: Test 1

problems.title: Data Type Behaviors On Change
problems.description: Data Type Behaviors on Change Use the sample setup shown below and match the example output. Implement the logic in main(...) and match the expected output for the provided cases.
problems.prompt: Data Type Behaviors on Change Use the sample setup shown below and match the example output.
problems.solution:
def main():
    def data_type_behaviors_on_change():
        print('\n--- Data Type Behaviors on Change ---')
        print('Description: This function demonstrates how different data types behave when they are modified.')

        def change_int(n):
            n += 10
            print('Integer value in function after modification:', n)
            return n

        def change_str(s):
            s = 'deepak mallick'
            print('String value in function after modification:', s)
            return s

        def change_list(lst):
            lst[0] = 0
            print('List value in function after modification:', lst)
            return lst

        def reassign_tuple(t):
            t = (4, 5, 6)
            print('Tuple value in function after reassignment:', t)
            return t

        def change_set(st):
            st.add(7)
            print('Set value in function after modification:', st)
            return st

        def reassign_set(st):
            st = {4, 5, 6}
            print('Set value in function after reassignment:', st)
            return st
        n = 5
        print('\nOriginal integer:', n)
        print('\nFunction:')
        change_int(n)
        print('Original Integer value:', n)
        s = 'deepak'
        print('\nOriginal string:', s)
        print('\nFunction:')
        change_str(s)
        print('Original String value:', s)
        lst = [1, 2, 3]
        print('\nOriginal list:', lst)
        print('\nFunction:')
        change_list(lst)
        print('Original List value:', lst)
        t = (1, 2, 3)
        print('\nOriginal tuple:', t)
        print('\nFunction:')
        reassign_tuple(t)
        print('Original Tuple value:', t)
        st = {1, 2, 3}
        print('\nOriginal set:', st)
        print('\nFunction:')
        change_set(st)
        print('Original Set value:', st)
        st = {1, 2, 3}
        print('\nOriginal set:', st)
        print('\nFunction:')
        reassign_set(st)
        print('Original Set value:', st)

    data_type_behaviors_on_change()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

examples.output:
--- Data Type Behaviors on Change ---
Description: This function demonstrates how different data types behave when they are modified.

Original integer: 5

Function:
Integer value in function after modification: 15
Original Integer value: 5

Original string: deepak

Function:
String value in function after modification: deepak mallick
Original String value: deepak

Original list: [1, 2, 3]

Function:
List value in function after modification: [0, 2, 3]
Original List value: [0, 2, 3]

Original tuple: (1, 2, 3)

Function:
Tuple value in function after reassignment: (4, 5, 6)
Original Tuple value: (1, 2, 3)

Original set: {1, 2, 3}

Function:
Set value in function after modification: {1, 2, 3, 7}
Original Set value: {1, 2, 3, 7}

Original set: {1, 2, 3}

Function:
Set value in function after reassignment: {4, 5, 6}
Original Set value: {1, 2, 3}

test_cases.expected_output:
